notes: Interface version means the advertised Wayland interface version, not the v1 or v2 protocol-generation suffix.
Only advertise the highest version whose complete request, event, lifecycle, error, and state semantics are implemented.
Privileged protocols require per-client global filtering or equivalent authorization before they are advertised.
blocked_by entries reference protocol IDs in this document and represent implementation dependencies, not protocol wire-level requirements.

protocols.status_detail: Surface and region behavior is implemented through v7, including compositor release and per-commit buffer release callbacks.
libwayland provides the v2 global, including release semantics; ARGB8888 and XRGB8888 buffers are copied into compositor-owned snapshots.

Version 4 is implemented for independent native DRM outputs, nested output, and headless output, including correct pixel modes, physical geometry, refresh updates, dynamic positions, connector hotplug, and output-global teardown.

Version 10 keyboard, pointer, and touch capabilities are implemented, including focus, client cursor surfaces, multi-contact touch routing, cancellation, frames, shape, orientation, nested input forwarding, and dynamically managed native input seats.

Clipboard and drag-and-drop are implemented through v4, including implicit-grab validation, drag icons, MIME negotiation and transfers, target routing, legacy interoperability, action negotiation, drop completion, and source/offer cancellation lifecycles.
Positioning, stacking, and synchronized and desynchronized commits are implemented.
The destroy_registry fix is implemented.
Version 7 is implemented, including toplevels, popups, positioners, configure and ack, grabs, repositioning, configure bounds, capabilities, suspended state, and version-gated constrained-edge state.
Preferred fractional scale tracks each output's configured native scale and updates dynamically from surface/output membership after layout changes.
Manager and toplevel-decoration lifecycles, mode negotiation, configure events, and initial-configure ordering are implemented through v2.

Version 6 imports single-plane linear ARGB8888 and XRGB8888 buffers into compositor-owned CPU snapshots with implicit synchronization, publishes sampling feedback, and validates sampling-device selection. Multi-plane formats and non-linear modifiers remain unadvertised optional capabilities.

Feedback is associated with exact applied, cached, and submitted surface commits. Nested mode forwards parent clock, timestamp, refresh, sequence, and timing flags; headless mode uses a monotonic nominal 60 Hz clock.

Version 1 issues unpredictable single-use tokens, validates optional seat serial and requesting-surface focus, and expires unused tokens. Activation consumes valid tokens but intentionally leaves focus policy unchanged until the compositor window manager gains an activation-request boundary.
Version 5 is implemented with per-output arrangement, exclusive usable-area mechanics, layer rendering and hit testing, popup parenting, and compositor-owned keyboard focus policy.

Version 2 follows exact keyboard-surface focus, implements multi-object enter/leave and single-active-input arbitration, double-buffers editor state, stages cursor rectangles through wl_surface commits, validates available actions, handles input-panel hints, and exposes input-method edit and state hooks.

Version 1 implements one input method per seat with unavailable competitors, text-input-v3 activation/state/edit brokerage and serial validation, physical keyboard grabs with stable press/release routing, and cursor-relative popup roles, placement, rendering, input, and lifecycle handling. The privileged global is hidden from sandboxed security-context clients.

Version 1 validates bounded NUL-terminated XKB keymaps, contributes keyboard capability only after keymap setup, injects tracked key and modifier state into the focused client, releases held keys on destruction, and bypasses input-method keyboard grabs to avoid feeding synthetic input back to its source. The privileged global is hidden from sandboxed security-context clients.

Version 2 implements wl_pointer and tablet-tool shape devices, validates shape availability by bound version, follows pointer capability and tablet-tool lifetimes, enforces enter/proximity serials, supports dynamic seats, and renders all 36 named shapes from XCURSOR_THEME and XCURSOR_SIZE with default-theme fallback.
Source, device, offer, MIME transfer, focus propagation, selection replacement, and cancellation lifecycles are implemented through v1.

Version 1 creates focus-sharing relative pointers tied to stable wl_pointer lifetimes, emits microsecond timestamps with accelerated and unaccelerated libinput deltas before absolute cursor clipping, and forwards native relative-pointer events from a supporting parent compositor in nested mode.

Version 1 supports focus-gated persistent and one-shot pointer locks and confinement, commit-buffered region and cursor-hint state, effective input-region intersection, and non-rectangular edge-sliding confinement. Locked pointers suppress absolute motion while relative motion continues; native pointer accumulation is synchronized after clipping.

Version 1 supports any number of surface-scoped inhibitors, removes their policy effect immediately when either the inhibitor or wl_surface is destroyed, and exposes live inhibitor state for visibility-aware idle, locking, screensaver, and display-power policy. Keywork does not yet initiate automatic idle actions, so there is currently nothing further to suppress.

Version 3 reports each output's logical position, scaled logical size, stable name, and description, refreshes configuration changes, makes bound resources inert on hotplug, and emits version-correct done events.

Version 2 interfaces at version 1 export xdg_toplevels with cryptographically random 128-bit handles, support repeated cross-client imports, apply the same mapped-parent and cycle semantics as xdg_toplevel.set_parent, and revoke owned relationships when imported/exported objects or target toplevels disappear. Direct and foreign child-parent references are also cleared when a parent window is destroyed.

Version 1 enforces one content-type object per wl_surface, keeps photo/video/game/none hints in the surface's pending, current, and synchronized-subsurface cached state, resets pending state to none when the object is destroyed, and makes objects inert when their wl_surface disappears.
Version 1 creates immutable premultiplied RGBA buffers, snapshots them through the shared surface buffer path, and supports viewporter scaling.

Version 1 validates and owns listening sockets, commits immutable sandbox-engine/application/instance metadata, accepts context-tagged Wayland clients until close-fd hangup, preserves listeners after the creating client disconnects, tracks metadata for each accepted client until disconnect, prevents nested security contexts, and centrally filters every implemented privileged global from sandboxed clients.

Version 1 implements exclusive lock acquisition and orphaned-lock recovery, one exact-size lock surface per output, configure and role validation, locked-frame presentation barriers across all outputs, secure blanking, hotplug and output reconfiguration, lock-only rendering and input routing, and explicit unlock. Sandboxed security-context clients cannot see the privileged global; input-method grabs and virtual keyboards are inhibited while locked.

Version 2 provides seat-scoped idle and input-idle notifications using a shared monotonic timer. Keyboard, pointer, scroll, and touch activity resume and restart matching notifications; input-idle notifications ignore inhibitors, while regular notifications track visible idle-inhibited surfaces and restart their timeout when inhibition ends.

Version 1 enforces one inhibitor per surface and seat, activates inhibition only while that exact surface has keyboard focus on the requested seat, and follows focus, grab, keyboard-capability, surface-destruction, and protocol-resource lifetimes. Active inhibitors bypass built-in compositor bindings while preserving press-time key ownership across focus changes; emergency compositor exit and virtual-terminal switching shortcuts remain available as the protocol permits.

Version 3 forwards libinput swipe, pinch, and hold gestures to the focused pointer client on each native input seat. Gesture routing remains bound to its starting seat, enforces one active gesture per seat, resets idle notifications, and sends cancellation when a device moves or disappears.

Stable version 2 advertises seat-scoped tablets, tools, and pads. Tool proximity, axes, tip, buttons, independent cursor surfaces, and cursor-shape-v1 integration are implemented alongside pad buttons, groups, modes, rings, strips, and version 2 dials. Pad focus follows the newest eligible tablet tool, prefers a tablet sharing the physical device, and supports standalone pads; hotplug, dynamic seat reassignment, session locking, surface destruction, and framed event delivery are handled. Feedback descriptions are accepted and intentionally ignored.

Version 1 provides privileged per-seat clipboard and primary-selection monitoring and replacement, MIME-validated transfers, source-use errors, stale-offer invalidation, cancellation, dynamic-seat teardown, and sandboxed-client global filtering.

Deprecated compatibility protocol version 2 shares the ext-data-control selection state and provides regular and primary clipboard monitoring, replacement, transfer, cancellation, dynamic-seat teardown, version 1 client compatibility, and sandboxed-client global filtering.

Version 1 advertises every mapped xdg-toplevel and managed X11 window with a never-reused opaque identifier, initial and updated title/app-id metadata, atomic done events, unmap/remap and close lifecycles, stopped-list handling, and sandboxed-client global filtering.

Deprecated compatibility protocol version 3 shares the ext foreign-toplevel mapping inventory for xdg-toplevel and managed X11 windows. It provides title/app-id, parent, state, output membership, close, activation, maximize, minimize, fullscreen, rectangle validation, stopped-manager teardown, and sandboxed-client global filtering. Close, activation, maximize, minimize, fullscreen, and state reporting work for both XDG and X11 windows.

Version 1 advertises one workspace group per output and the active or occupied numbered workspaces (1 through 10), with one-dimensional ordering, active-state synchronization, output hotplug, late wl_output bindings, atomic done events, and inert stopped-manager handles. Workspace activation is applied atomically on manager commit; create, remove, deactivate, and assignment capabilities are intentionally not advertised for the fixed per-output model. The privileged global is hidden from sandboxed security-context clients.

Version 4 head discovery, EDID make/model/serial identity, complete DRM mode advertisement and selection, hotplug lifecycles, stale-serial cancellation, complete test/apply transactions, positions, fractional scales, and native DRM enable/disable are implemented for Kanshi. Mode and scale changes update scanout buffers, logical geometry, rendering, input bounds, wl_output, xdg-output, fractional-scale, layer-shell, and compositor window-management state, with transactional rollback on failure. Unsupported custom modes, transforms, and adaptive sync are rejected. The privileged global is hidden from sandboxed security-context clients.

Version 1 provides exclusive per-output DRM power controls with immediate initial and changed mode events, invalid-mode errors, and failed/inert handling for duplicate controls, unsupported or removed outputs, and hardware failures. Powering off suspends scanout and rendering while preserving wl_output and compositor layout state; powering on recreates scanout buffers and schedules a fresh frame. The privileged global is hidden from sandboxed security-context clients.

Version 1 creates persistent opaque sources for live wl_output resources and mapped ext-foreign-toplevel handles. Sources survive factory and foreign-handle destruction, become inert when their output is removed or their toplevel unmaps, and expose source invalidation to capture consumers. Both privileged factory globals are hidden from sandboxed security-context clients.

Version 1 synchronously captures complete output scenes, mapped toplevel surface trees, and dedicated pointer cursor images into ARGB8888/XRGB8888 wl_shm buffers or CPU-mappable linear DMA-BUFs. DMA-BUF constraints advertise the allocation device, formats, and modifiers when available, with stride, offset, and Y-inversion respected during writes. Output sessions honor paint-cursors. Cursor sessions track image intersection with enter/leave events, report scale-aware position and hotspot metadata, update constraints when cursor dimensions change, and include cursor-surface subsurfaces. Captures preserve session/frame lifetime rules, source invalidation stops sessions, and frame metadata includes full conservative damage plus monotonic timestamps. The privileged global is security-context filtered.

Version 3 captures complete outputs and clipped logical output regions into ARGB8888 wl_shm buffers or CPU-mappable linear ARGB8888 DMA-BUFs. Cursor overlay requests, copy and copy-with-damage, full conservative damage, output invalidation, frame timestamps, buffer validation, and Y-inversion flags are supported. Fractional-scale region boundaries match the corresponding physical pixels in full-output captures. The privileged global is security-context filtered.

Version 1 private-global filtering, the xwayland_surface role, serial association, rootless Xwayland launch, XWM/Composite/EWMH bootstrap, and X11 window lifecycle tracking are implemented. Managed X11 windows participate directly in the compositor-owned workspace and layout policy, including tiling, focus, visibility, stacking, fullscreen, maximize, minimize, activation, and foreign-toplevel controls. Auxiliary window types bypass tiling while desktop windows remain at the bottom and transients remain above parents. ICCCM/EWMH focus, close, state, clipboard, primary selection, and bidirectional Xdnd transfers are supported. Further X11 policy compatibility is best-effort and is not part of protocol completion.

Version 1 is exposed as a private global only to the compositor-launched Xwayland client. Active grabs route physical key and modifier events to the requested Xwayland surface independently of normal keyboard focus, preserve press/release ownership and repeat behavior, and restore the underlying focus on teardown. Grabs yield to input-method keyboard grabs and are canceled before a session lock takes focus.

Version 5 implements shell and per-surface resource lifecycles, validated startup and D-Bus metadata, modal state, conservative focus and bell policy, version-correct tiled and resizable-edge configures synchronized before each XDG toplevel configure, the release destructor, and titlebar gesture enum, seat, and pointer-serial validation. No global menu, menu bar, or desktop-icon capabilities are advertised.

Version 1 is advertised only when a usable DRM timeline-syncobj device supports eventfd waits and sync-file transfer. It implements timeline import, per-surface acquire and release points, protocol validation and teardown, acquire-gated DMA-BUF commits within the existing ordered commit cache, and release signaling after all renderer and direct-scanout users finish. The lifecycle is exercised end to end with both CPU and Vulkan renderers.

Version 1 implements the manager and inert per-surface lifecycle, duplicate-object errors, and double-buffered hints preserved through cached synchronized commits. Fullscreen async-hinted windows use DRM asynchronous page flips for composited and direct-scanout frames when the active legacy or atomic KMS path reports support, with synchronous fallback on rejection and accurate presentation feedback; session-lock and non-DRM outputs remain synchronized.

Version 1 implements per-surface lifecycle and errors plus one-shot, double-buffered set and wait barriers integrated with ordered cached commits and explicit synchronization. Visible barriers bind to an output, force the following latch to use non-tearing presentation, and unblock waiting commits only after the frozen frame is submitted and attributed; synchronized-subsurface waits are ignored as required, and output removal guarantees progress. Multi-refresh FIFO sequencing is exercised end to end with CPU and Vulkan renderers.

Version 1 implements manager and per-surface timer lifecycles and errors plus one-shot presentation-clock timestamps integrated as a readiness gate in the ordered commit cache. Future targets use a shared event-loop timer with rounded-up scheduling, preserve role, explicit-sync, FIFO, and synchronized-subsurface ordering, and cannot be bypassed by later untimed commits. Protocol errors and delayed multi-commit presentation are exercised end to end with CPU and Vulkan renderers.

Version 1 implements exclusive pre-drag data-source reservation, manager and drag lifecycles and errors, mapped and newly mapped toplevel attachment, automatic detachment on unmap or destruction, and movement termination on drop or cancellation. Attached windows are raised, moved through persistent floating placement with geometry-relative offsets, and excluded with their popups, decorations, and borders from drag target hit testing. Source, manager, and replacement-toplevel lifecycles are exercised end to end.

Version 1 implements named and multi-scale SHM icon objects, square-buffer validation, size-and-scale replacement, immutable assignment, destroyed-buffer errors, and manager-independent child lifetimes. Assignments are deep-copied into double-buffered XDG toplevel metadata on surface commit, so icons persist after their source icon and buffers are destroyed; null and empty icons restore the default. The compositor advertises no preferred icon sizes. Protocol errors, replacement, persistence, and manager destruction are exercised end to end.

Version 1 implements one dialog object per XDG toplevel, dialog and modal metadata updates, effect removal on dialog destruction, inert objects after toplevel destruction, and manager-independent child lifetimes. Dialog hints persist across unmap and combine with existing parent-relative transient placement and stacking; clients retain the protocol-defined responsibility for filtering parent events. Duplicate construction, recreation, manager destruction, and inert-object requests are exercised end to end.

Version 1 advertises the system-bell global and accepts bell requests with either an associated surface or no surface. Requests follow the protocol-permitted no-feedback policy, matching the existing GTK system-bell behavior until Keywork gains a shared audible or visual notification subsystem. Null, toplevel-associated, and object-destruction lifecycles are exercised end to end.

Version 1 stores allocator-owned untranslated tags and translated descriptions as XDG toplevel metadata, supports replacement at any point in the toplevel lifetime, preserves values across unmap, and notifies metadata observers for future policy and session-management consumers. Tag, description, replacement, manager destruction, and toplevel destruction are exercised end to end.

Version 1 implements durable session creation, takeover, removal, toplevel add/restore/remove/rename, and inert resource lifecycles. Per-instance state survives compositor restarts under XDG_RUNTIME_DIR and restores output/workspace, floating geometry, maximized, fullscreen, and minimized state before the initial XDG configure. Creation, rename, manager independence, cross-restart session and toplevel restoration ordering, and removal are exercised end to end.

Version 1 lets privileged clients create independent capability-free wl_seat globals with unique names. Seats are removed with their transient handles, become inert for attached virtual keyboards, and remain allocated only while existing seat resources drain. The manager is hidden from security-context clients, and creation, binding, capability updates through virtual-keyboard-v1, global removal, inert input, child-resource draining, and compositor shutdown are exercised end to end.

Version 2 implements privileged virtual pointers for the default and transient seats, including optional output mapping, relative and absolute motion, balanced multi-device buttons, axis source/stop/discrete/value120 events, and frame delivery. Physical and virtual pointer capabilities compose without invalidating live resources, and transient-seat removal makes attached devices inert. Manager-v2 mapping, XDG surface routing, pointer events, pressed-button teardown, manager independence, transient-seat cleanup, and compositor shutdown are exercised end to end.

Version 1 validates enter serials against the requested active wl_pointer, requires pointer focus on an in-bounds surface owned by the same client, and warps logical and native cursor state without emitting synthetic pointer motion. Per-resource serial tracking preserves wl_pointer lifetime and capability-generation boundaries. Valid, wrong-serial, out-of-bounds, no-motion, subsequent-motion, and implicit-grab behavior are exercised end to end.

Version 1 always advertises blur availability, enforces one effect object per wl_surface, copies and double-buffers blur regions through synchronized subsurface commits, makes effect objects inert when their surface disappears, and blurs only requested regions with compositor-defined parameters.
